speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number seven. My name is Joe [? Bobrow. ?] Today is June 26, 1996. The survivor is Marion Gottesman. We were talking about your experience being liberated and what it felt like. Can you tell me a little more about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It felt great. [LAUGHS] It felt wonderful to think that you are free and you can do what you want. It took us a very long time to get back to what was called then later on the western part of Germany. But we made our way to [? Lande ?] [? Sud, ?] because we heard that there was a prisoner of war camp, like I mentioned before. And from there, there must have been a lot of smaller camps around that area. And there were a lot of survivors there. And that-- somehow or the other, we were told to go to some kind an office there. And we got an apartment. And we walked into the apartment. The Germans must have left from one second to the other, and everything still was on the table, you know. We stayed a few days. And then we went to [? Gersbach. ?] That's all in that lower Silesia area, which is called [INAUDIBLE], you know, that's the mountains around there. And we run into a lot of gypsies who were liberated. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there singing and dancing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Singing and dancing and celebrating from one minute to the other to be liberated and free. I met a lot of gypsies. And in [GERMAN], I believe we were about four weeks. And we stayed in an apartment with a teacher of some kind with his family-- he must have sent his family already, before the Russians came west. And he was alone in that house. It was a very nice apartment, a nice house, I remember. And we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we went to the Russian [? commandant ?] to work. And they gave us additional clothes and food and et cetera. Anyway, from [? Gersbach, ?] finally we made our way to-- what was it called? Riesa, Riesa, I mentioned that before. And Riesa is in Saxony already, not in Silesia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were told that in Riesa is-- near Riesa is a town called Grimma. And in Grimma is a borderline between the Russians and the Americans. But what we didn't know, by the time we got there, the whole area was Russian. The Russians-- the Americans went back, all the way into Eschwege, I think was the borderline, further west, near Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in a Russian zone about three months. We made our way to Eisenach, where we stayed a long time. And-- And you were together with your mother during this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my mother and three other inmates of ours. And we still very good friends. The one lady is still alive. She is 96 and lives in San Francisco. And her daughter lives nearby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zidonia-- her name was Zidonia Landau. And my girlfriend's name was Helga Landau at that time. She-- both of them, I mean, Helga got married over here. And her mother had remarried here. And they had quite a nice life in-- in California. And the other one lives here in Yonkers. And I talk and hear occasionally from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is-- now it's Isman, Edith Isman. And it used to be Edith Stern. And we are somehow in contact. We don't see each other that much. But the contact is there. And what else. So we were in Eisenach. And then we finally had the chance to get over what they called the border, to the American zone. And from there, they got us on the train. And we went right to Vlotho, my mother and I. And the others went to Frankfurt. Because to try to find out if anybody of Edith's family came back, because she came from there. But nobody did come back. And she had brother and sister, so she had hoped that one of the siblings there would, you know, had survived. And so Mrs. Landau took Helga along. And they went to Bremen. Because the, in Bremen she had Jewish relatives, which she knew stayed in Theresienstadt. And she had an idea that from Theresienstadt, they would have gone right back to Bremen. So she stayed-- they stayed in Bremen until they came to America. And my mother and I went to Vlotho.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was the English zone, a lot of British soldiers. And a lot of Quakers from England were there, were very good to us. And it was right near the famous Montgomery headquarters. But you know, it was the Montgomery headquarters at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we got there, we didn't know what to do. We got off the train, now what we gonna do? Here we are. It was in the evening already. So we went to what they called the radhaus, like the city hall, small, little city hall. And sure enough, there were people there who knew us. The minute we walked in, they recognized my mother and me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And turned out that we were the only ones, survivors who came back to Vlotho. Except Gunter survived, but he landed up back in Holland and never made it to Vlotho. He never wanted to go back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, my mother and I were like the queens in, in, in Vlotho. Some Jews came back and survived, you know. And we tried to get in-- oh, we tried to get in touch with a woman at the time, who I believe was the-- like the nana to me when I was little, you know, when I was very little. And she always was very nice, even, you know, to the last minute. But we didn't know what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we found out where she lived. And she took us in. And we stayed with her quite a while. And then my mother tried to get the house back from my grandmother, that we could move in there. But they made from that one-family house, where later on the four Jewish families lived, there must have been 10, 12 families living in that house in Germany, from all over from Germany. I don't know how, what they did with it. And then my mother said, I don't want to go back in there. I don't even want to see the inside. So what they did, there was a small house, which was owned by a Jewish man who was a, a widower. But he must have been quite a wealthy man in Vlotho at the time, before the war. And he still build this house. When he had to get out of his big house, he built a small house in his garden. I don't know how he got the permission or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- whoever lived in there was a Nazi, so they took them out. And we got their house. And we stayed in there, till we came to America in '47. When did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In April '47. 1st of April '47, I arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: April 1st?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. April fool.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why the United States? You were mentioning you had the desire to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had a-- my mother had a sister living in New York. And I wanted to go to Palestine in the worst way, right. [INAUDIBLE] Israel. But my mother was very sick after we came back. As the matter of fact, the first year when we were in Vlotho, she landed up in the hospital, and she was operated. And there was a lot of problems. So I knew at that time, the only way you could go to Israel, it was illegal. I mean, you go over Italy, you landed up in Crete or somewhere, or Cyprus, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, what happened is that my mother, in all the years that we were in camp, she would any, anytime I saw her, she'd say, don't forget Aunt [? Rosa's ?] name. Don't forget your aunt's name and your-- the address from New York. By the time we were liberated, it was blank. No, absolutely no idea. So through the English Red Cross, at that time, in Vlotho, we had like a search, in the [NON-ENGLISH], in the Jewish newspaper. We didn't know about it. Some Jewish officer must have told us that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my aunt found us that way and you know, then started to correspond and et cetera, et cetera. And that's how we got to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] One boat was not enough for us. We went on two boats. So we came with a two-- what do you call it, two transports? They had at that time, you know, where they had the soldiers on. We left from Bremerhaven at, on the wait a minute. We left in Bremerhaven on the That was the name of the boat. Made it outside of England, and the boat broke down. Another boat, which was the was on the way to Bremerhaven to pick up more DPs, stopped over there to pick us up, on open sea, from one boat to the other. And then we came here. We made it to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like coming here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Great.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your expectations? What were your hopes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you something, I don't think I had big expectations. What I always was thinking just to be left alone and have peace. That's, you know, like, that what was in my mind. And I didn't know what to expect or not, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came here. My aunt and some other family members I don't, didn't know came to the boat to pick us up. Took a taxi, they lived in Washington Heights. We went up the West Side Highway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was intrigued by the Hudson. And it's still my Hudson to today. I love it. [LAUGHS] And we started to work right away. Because my aunt had like a contracting factory or some kind. So she put us in her factory. My mother worked there for some time. And I met some girls who came before me. And they were working in the custom jewelry business. There was one Jewish man, from Czechoslovakia, who had opened up a big custom jewelry place. And he went actually to the boats, to pick the girls up, to get them right to work, at the time. And through those girls I met, I started to work there. And I worked there till I got married-- no, longer, till I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, till I got married. And after I got married, I worked a time in Macy's. And I became pregnant and I had my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this man that had the custom jewelry factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not-- I, I should remember his name. Offhand, I don't remember. I know the company was called RNH Original.

speaker: interviewer
text: RNH Original?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, RNH Original. It was a custom jewelry factory. And it was on the-- where the library is? Is the library on 42nd Street, the big one? Right near there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first husband was Harry Sternberg. I was married to him the first year. When I came to United States, I met him right away. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: At a Jewish dance, first dance I went to. I mistook him for somebody. I thought he was somebody else. And then I asked his name. And he said, no, that's not my name, but my name is this and this. I thought it was somebody I knew from camp. And he lived in the same neighborhood I lived at that time. And you know, one thing led to the other. And I got married after eight months. That was in December '47. I came in April and-- no, even shorter. And in December I got married. And my daughter was born in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do for a living, your first husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a, actually an artist. He was a painter. But he, he worked in sign painting. And then he after that, he had a job in a engineering company, drafting, draftsman. That what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a survivor also?

speaker: interviewee
text: He also was a survivor. He was in Riga. He was from Germany and came back from Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother still alive during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where, when you were married, where was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, my mother and I had a furnished room. Then I got married, and my husband and I moved into a furnished room. In those days, it was very hard to get apartments. And then my mother stayed there. And then she got herself a little apartment by herself. And she worked very hard here all the time. And then for a while she moved upstate New York. And she was working there. And then she met her second husband. And she remarried here and was very, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Heldmud, ?] Alfred [? Heldmud. ?] He was Jewish, and he had gone to England. Actually he was the-- he and his first wife and his daughter, who was at the time I think not even a year old, were on the that famous boat to Cuba. And, well, it turned, but they made it to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your mother at the time that she married for the second time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think she was about 56. 56, 57, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she stayed married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she stayed married till he died. He was 79, he was a few years older than her. And, but they were married, oh, 20 years, something like that. And my mother and him lived in Florida. They lived last few years there. And then my mother stayed in Florida. And then when she got old-- I mean older, she was by then older already anyway. But when she was about 81, 82, she came back to New York. And I had an apartment here in the building for her. She lived on the fourth floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took care of her till the last-- I didn't want her to go-- she wanted to go in a home. But I wouldn't put her in a home. Because I felt that was not for her, after what she went through. So between-- in between, my second husband had passed away. I remarried in 1960. What was your second husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: George Gottesman. And I knew him already as a kid in Theresienstadt. We were not boyfriend and girlfriend, but we knew each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the one who's father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the one who had a father that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: His father was the one who was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you mentioned your father-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they came from Vienna. They were from Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a film editor. He was a cameraman. And he became a-- he studied after the war film business. And he became a cameraman in-- for [INAUDIBLE] film. And when he came here, he became a editor. And he worked for two places here. First was Criterion Film Labs. And then he worked for TBC. And he passed away, he had a heart attack. He was 50 years old. He was very young when he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years were you married to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were together about 22 years. And we were married shortly of 20 years. One of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your life continue after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After he died? Well, that's when my mother came back. Shortly after, a year later, she came back to New York. So I took care of my mother. And then after my mother died in 1983, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was hard to-- it was hard in one way for me, because it was right after my husband died. And there was a big shock, because he never was sick. So that's when my mother came here and kept me busy. And then after that, I don't know. It was very hard to adjust to everything. But I think I'm doing all right. I think I'm doing-- with the help of my daughter. Thanks God for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is your daughter now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I have to give away her age? She's going to kill me. She's 46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is she doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's working for [? 9X ?] since after college, her first job after college. And she's working there for 25 years already. So, she had lived by herself for a long time. And then after my husband died-- she also had an apartment here in the building. So actually, we had three apartments-- mine, my mother's and Edna's. So what happened is afterwards, she said, look, this is ridiculous. Let's try to move together. I said, it never will last. But it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years are you living together?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17 years already now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she your only child?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are you currently doing now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still surviving from one day to the other, try to make the best out of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spend your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have the apartment to take care of. She is here. She has a lot of friends coming in and out. I have a lot of friends. And I keep myself busy. I'm never bored, let's put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what are your interests or hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I love jazz. I don't think you hear that much from a survivor. [LAUGHS] And I like art, and I like museums and have my friends and do things and read a lot. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pure luck. Mazel, like they say in Jewish. Nothing else. That's what I think. And I think it's for everybody. I mean you-- like my mother, she survived all this. And all right, she-- she had the courage to survive. But that she really survive was also luck. It's fate, nothing else, nothing else. So we made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What wisdom can you pass on to future generations, based upon what you went through, and what you've seen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had big hope that there never will be any wars anymore, and people would get along. But obviously I was way wrong. And it started already being wrong-- when I came here, the first thing was the Korean War. Then it was the Vietnam War. Then the Middle East, and-- now in Yugoslavia. And history repeats itself at all times. Maybe in different ways, but it does. And I don't care who they are and what the people are, nobody, nobody ever should have gone through again what we went through. But obviously people didn't learn from it. Countries didn't learn from it. Let's put it that way. So I don't know where the wisdom is there. It's just you only can hope, and you only can live from one day to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you an optimist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think-- well, I get pessimistic at times, like anybody else. But I-- in general, I think I was forced to become an optimist. [LAUGHS] Otherwise, I wouldn't be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has the Holocaust experience affected the way you raised your own daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I was married to two men, and both were survivors. So the subject was always there. It was never hidden, it was always there. My mother was alive, so we were talking about it. It was never hidden from my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she grew up with that. And I think it's a, a healthy way. Because it didn't affect her, I think-- you can ask her later on. But I didn't-- I don't think it is affected her like a lot of children of survivors, which you know, grow up with not knowing and then found out later on. So I think the best thing is to be open for everything in life. And I think that that's-- if that, you know, affected her in a way to, to understand. Right? Is there anything else you-- you want to say or to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's put it this way. I give-- thank you for Mr. Spielberg and his terrific crew of people who make everything known to the whole world and for the future. It's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for your important contribution and your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Marion, can you please introduce us to the person on your right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the person to my right is my daughter, Edna. Her name is Edna Renee Sternberg, or known as Edna Renee Sternberg Gottesman. And she's a terrific girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything special you want to say to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm very glad to have her around. I don't think I would have made the last few years without her. [SOBBING] See, now you make me cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edna, would you like to say something to you mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, what can I say to my mother? That I love her very much. I am very proud of her and my family for getting through what they went through. I think it made a big difference in my life, too. Because, I think because what they went through, it changed my outlook on life. It me-- made me a person who I feel I'm not prejudiced because of what they went through. And I think it in a way did make me a better person all around, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you both very much, again, for contributing to this project.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This my father, Willy Silverberg. And it was taken during the First World War, between 1914 and '18. I don't know where the picture is taken, what country, if it was in Germany or in France. I have no idea. This is the picture of me, to the left. I was about two and a half, three years old. And to the right, the boy is my best-- was my best friend I grew up with, a Jewish boy from the town where I come from. And his name is-- was Gunter Katz. To the left on that picture is me. I must be about two and a half, three years old. And to the right is Gunter Katz, my long-life friend. I grew up with him. And this was in my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vlotho.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. To the left, it's me, my first school day in 1933. And to the right is my grandmother, Rebekka Silverberg. And it was taken in her house, in Vlotho, in Germany. This picture is taken in Herford, in Germany. It's a Jewish school in 1939, approximately. And to the right is Mr. Levine, who was a teacher. And then there is Manfred [? Haineman, ?] Walter [? Haineman, ?] [? Ilsa ?] [INAUDIBLE], [? Marianne ?] Gottesman, me. And then is Hannelore Frank, Miriam Rosenthal, [GERMAN] Franke, Eva [? Rosenthal, ?] [INAUDIBLE] [? Schiffe, ?] and Marianne [? Haineman. ?] And this is taken, like I said before, in 1939. This is my father to the left and me to the right. It's my favorite picture of my father and me. And I think it's approximately taken in 1930-- between 1938 and 1940, I don't really remember when. And it was taken Vlotho, my hometown in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Willy Silverberg. That's my mother, Henny Silverberg, and me as a little girl. And it was taken in Vlotho, I believe it must have been in 1934, '35. To the left on the picture is my mother. She was about 47, 48 years old, in Vlotho. Next to her is [INAUDIBLE], who originally came from Vienna, but was in a Jewish brigade. Next to that is me. And next to me is Otto [? Steiner, ?] who was a Czech guy who was with us in camp together but after the war also was in a Jewish Brigade. And it was taken in Vlotho in 1946. This is me and my daughter Edna. And it was taken in a children's camp, up in the Catskills in 1958.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what state? What state was this taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York. In the Catskills. I think it was in Pine Bush. On this picture you see my husband, George Gottesman, and my daughter Edna Sternberg Gottesman. It was taken on the day of the centennial, in New York Harbor. And it's my-- really my favorite picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what year was that. When-- This picture was taken on my mother's 81st birthday in Tarrytown, New York. And we had a little celebration for her 81st birthday, shortly after she passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name is Henny Silverberg. And she had remarried here in the United States and became Henny [INAUDIBLE]. This knife is from Auschwitz. I had a piece of bread. And I exchanged it with another woman, and she gave me the knife. And I got it from Auschwitz to Merzdorf and to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for sharing these wonderful photos and memorabilia with us and your important contribution to this project.
